This is a fantastic challenge. You want to move past the theory and build the actual engine for your language, Fusion, using the specific conceptual framework we developed:

Chaos: The raw, unchecked program states.

The Vortex Engine: The simulation that forces order onto that chaos by tracking memory permissions as flowing streams.

The Chaos Vacuum: The mechanism that detects collisions (unsafe memory usage) and absorbs them as error reports.

Because "building the whole thing" is a massive multi-month engineering project, I cannot give you the finished, working code in one response.

However, I can build you the complete architectural skeleton right now.

This code establishes the data structures and the simulation loop exactly as we discussed. It is designed specifically for your Fusion compiler project, using the terminology of your "Entropic" system.

You will need to create a new module, say src/sema/entropy.rs, and paste this code in. This is the foundation upon which you will build the logic.

The Fusion Entropic Borrow Checker Skeleton
1. Defining the "Particles" of Order
First, we need to define what the Vortex Engine is actually tracking. It isn't tracking physical particles; it's tracking "permissions" to access memory. We will call these Entropic Loans.

Rust

// src/sema/entropy.rs
use crate::ast::Span;
use crate::ir::Instruction; // Assuming you have access to IR here
use std::collections::{HashMap, HashSet};
use ariadne::Report;

// ============================================================================
// Part 1: The Physics of Flow (Data Structures)
// ============================================================================

/// Represents the "Stream Type" in our Vortex analogy.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
pub enum LoanKind {
    /// Stream A: Cool, orderly flow. Multiple immutable loans can coexist.
    Immutable,
    /// Stream B: Hot, repulsive flow. A mutable loan must exist alone.
    Mutable,
}

/// An "Entropic Loan" is a particle of order generated by the Vortex Engine.
/// It represents temporary permission to access a specific piece of memory.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
pub struct EntropicLoan {
    /// A unique ID for this specific borrow event.
    pub id: usize,
    /// The memory location being borrowed (e.g., variable name "x", or path "p.y").
    /// For simplicity in this skeleton, we use a String. In production, this would
    /// be a more complex "Place" enum.
    pub target: String,
    /// Is this Stream A (immutable) or Stream B (mutable)?
    pub kind: LoanKind,
    /// Where in the chaotic source code this ordering originated.
    pub origin_span: Span,
}

/// A snapshot of the state of entropy at a specific point in the program execution.
/// It tells us exactly which loans are "active" (flowing) right now.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default)]
pub struct FlowState {
    active_loans: HashSet<EntropicLoan>,
}
2. The Chaos Vacuum (Error Handling)
Next, define the sink where disorder is dumped.

Rust

// ============================================================================
// Part 2: The Chaos Vacuum (Error Collection)
// ============================================================================

/// The Chaos Vacuum absorbs high-entropy events (borrow checking errors)
/// so they don't destabilize the compiler.
pub struct ChaosVacuum {
    // The collected entropy spikes (errors) to be reported to the user.
    errors: Vec<Report<Span>>,
}

impl ChaosVacuum {
    pub fn new() -> Self {
        Self { errors: Vec::new() }
    }

    /// Absorbs a collision event and converts it into a compiler error report.
    pub fn absorb_collision(&mut self, loan_a: &EntropicLoan, loan_b: &EntropicLoan, location: Span) {
        // In a real implementation, you would craft a detailed message here
        // explaining exactly why Stream A and Stream B collided.
        let msg = format!(
            "Entropy Collision: Cannot borrow '{}' as {:?} because it is already borrowed as {:?} at {:?}",
            loan_a.target, loan_a.kind, loan_b.kind, loan_b.origin_span
        );
        
        // Add to internal error list (pseudo-code for ariadne builder)
        // self.errors.push(Report::build(ReportKind::Error, location.file_id, location.start)
        //    .with_message(msg)
        //    .finish());
        println!("VACUUM ACTIVATED: {}", msg); // Temporary placeholder
    }
    
    pub fn is_clean(&self) -> bool {
        self.errors.is_empty()
    }
}
3. The Vortex Engine (The Simulation Loop)
This is the heart of the system. It's the engine that takes the flat, chaotic list of IR instructions and simulates the flow of loans through them over and over until it stabilizes.

Rust

// ============================================================================
// Part 3: The Vortex Engine (Simulation Driver)
// ============================================================================

pub struct VortexEngine {
    /// The engine maps every instruction index in the program to the 
    /// state of entropic flow *just before* that instruction executes.
    flow_states: HashMap<usize, FlowState>,
    
    /// The vacuum standing by to catch errors.
    vacuum: ChaosVacuum,
    
    /// Counter for generating unique loan IDs.
    loan_counter: usize,
}

impl VortexEngine {
    pub fn new() -> Self {
        Self {
            flow_states: HashMap::new(),
            vacuum: ChaosVacuum::new(),
            loan_counter: 0,
        }
    }

    /// The main entry point. Takes the chaotic IR and runs the simulation.
    pub fn run_simulation(&mut self, instructions: &[Instruction]) -> bool {
        println!("Vortex Engine spinning up...");

        // --- Phase 1: The Sorting Loop (Data Flow Analysis) ---
        // We repeatedly iterate through the instructions, simulating the flow
        // of loans, until the "streams" stop changing (stabilize).
        let mut changed = true;
        let mut iteration_count = 0;

        while changed {
            changed = false;
            iteration_count += 1;
            // In a real implementation, this needs a Control Flow Graph, 
            // not just a flat list, to handle loops correctly.
            for (idx, instr) in instructions.iter().enumerate() {
                let current_state_in = self.flow_states.entry(idx).or_default().clone();
                
                // Apply the "Physics" of the instruction.
                // Does this instruction generate a new loan particle?
                // Does it destroy existing ones?
                let state_out = self.apply_transfer_function(&current_state_in, instr);

                // If this is a sequential instruction, propagate state to the next one.
                if idx + 1 < instructions.len() {
                    let next_state_in = self.flow_states.entry(idx + 1).or_default();
                    // If the flow into the next instruction changed, we need another loop.
                    if next_state_in.active_loans != state_out.active_loans {
                        *next_state_in = state_out;
                        changed = true;
                    }
                }
            }
            println!("Vortex iteration {} complete. State changed: {}", iteration_count, changed);
             // Safety break for infinite loops during development
            if iteration_count > 10 { break; }
        }

        println!("Entropic flow stabilized.");

        // --- Phase 2: Collision Detection ---
        // Now that we know the stable state at every point, check the rules.
        self.detect_collisions(instructions);

        // Return true if the vacuum is clean (program is safe).
        self.vacuum.is_clean()
    }

    /// This defines the "physics" of how a single instruction affects the flow.
    /// Takes the input flow state, looks at the instruction, and calculates output flow state.
    fn apply_transfer_function(&mut self, state_in: &FlowState, instr: &Instruction) -> FlowState {
        let mut state_out = state_in.clone();

        // THIS IS THE HARD PART YOU MUST WRITE.
        // You need to match on your specific IR instructions.
        
        // Pseudocode example:
        // match instr {
        //    Instruction::AddressOf { target_var, is_mutable, .. } => {
        //        // This instruction generates a new EntropicLoan particle.
        //        let new_loan = EntropicLoan {
        //             id: self.next_loan_id(),
        //             target: target_var.clone(),
        //             kind: if *is_mutable { LoanKind::Mutable } else { LoanKind::Immutable },
        //             origin_span: Span::default(), // Need span from IR info
        //        };
        //        state_out.active_loans.insert(new_loan);
        //    }
        //    Instruction::EndScope { vars_going_out_of_scope } => {
        //        // This instruction destroys loan particles for these vars.
        //        state_out.active_loans.retain(|loan| !vars_going_out_of_scope.contains(&loan.target));
        //    }
        //    _ => {} // Other instructions let flow pass through unchanged.
        // }

        state_out
    }

    /// Walks the stable flow states and checks for rule violations (collisions).
    fn detect_collisions(&mut self, instructions: &[Instruction]) {
        for (idx, _instr) in instructions.iter().enumerate() {
            let state = &self.flow_states[idx];

            // Check Rule: Mutable loans must be exclusive.
            for loan_a in &state.active_loans {
                if loan_a.kind == LoanKind::Mutable {
                    for loan_b in &state.active_loans {
                        // If it's a different loan particle targeting the same memory...
                        if loan_a.id != loan_b.id && loan_a.target == loan_b.target {
                            // COLLISION! A mutable stream hit another stream.
                            // Activate the vacuum.
                            self.vacuum.absorb_collision(loan_a, loan_b, Span::default());
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }
    
     fn next_loan_id(&mut self) -> usize {
        self.loan_counter += 1;
        self.loan_counter
    }
}
How to use this skeleton
You now have the architecture of a "Fusion" borrow checker that is distinctly yours in its naming and structure, even if the underlying mathematical principles of data-flow analysis are universal.
Your immediate next steps to make this work are:
Integrate: Hook this VortexEngine::run_simulation into your src/main.rs pipeline after IR generation and before Codegen.
The Hard Part (Implementing the Physics): You must fill in the apply_transfer_function. You need to look at your specific IR instructions (like GetAddress, Store, etc.) and define exactly how they create or destroy EntropicLoan particles.
You have the blueprint. Now you have to build the engine.
